author: @vevo3130
comment: Let me tell you hebu niongee kwa Kiswahili, ni hivi imani na utamaduni ni moja kati ya vitu hatari sana Mwanadamu akishikilia; bila kutafakari au asipotumia akili, na ndio hii kitu wamasai wanafanya yaani kuna saa ukifikiria au ukiwauliza kwanini bado mwafanya hivyo? na ni ukatili hawana jibu lingine zaidi ya kuwa wanaenzi utamaduni au imani, ila ukweli ni kwa sababu Mwanadamu hapendi kuambiwa ni  ile akili ya uzuzu ya "huwezi niambia kitu wewe" hasa wanaume na wazee wa hizi jamii hasa kama hivyo mmeenda wanawake wengi ndio kabisaaaaa yaani "HAWAAMBILIKI" ukiwaangalia hapo ni kama wanaelewa na wamejua ila sio mara ya kwanza wanaambiwa na imagine miaka yote hiyo unadhani hawajui, wanajua vizuri na ndio maana wanafanya kwa kificho au wanafanyia watoto wachanga kabisa, wanajua ni makosa na ni kinyume na sheria; ila sasa kwa sababu ya kutoambilika "HAWAAMBILIKI" na sheria pia zimelegalega basi ndio kabisaaa..."mwanadamu hakomboleki mpaka aamue mwenyewe" so tuendelee tu kuwaelimisha ila maamuzi ni juu yao na watoto ni wao....NASIKITIKA SANA NA NAUMIA SANA WATOTO WAKIFANYIWA UKATILI

author: @silvanawathoni2176
comment:
The involvement of men in the fight against (FGM) is crucial for ending the practice. While FGM is often seen as a "women's issue," men play significant roles in decision-making, cultural traditions, and societal norms, making their engagement essential.

Men in this communuties practicing fgm  especially in the maasai community ,need to be informed about the physical, psychological, and emotional consequences of FGM.

author: @susan.wanjiku_
comment: Watching this has made me wonder how taken away some of us are from the reality of what’s happening on the ground. Like the privilege some of us have had growing up in families and communities that have protected you as a girl could make you super unaware of what other girls and women are going through in other communities. This has sensitized me too! To look into how I can get involved and educate myself and others too because honestly, huu ni unyama. It’s scary how prevalent it is. This was a very chilling documentary. God help these communities. More importantly, may we have the wisdom and the courage to really do something tangible about it, at whatever level we are capable of doing it from.

author: @angel.angel.636
comment: @silviangari3291  True, but traditions like foot-binding and slavery and many others were once accepted but later seen as cruel and unnecessary. So why does FGM still exist? A rite of passage should uplift, not harm. Young people today understand the risks and pain better than past generations, i mean most parents now bringing up this vulnerable group are young people. The fact that FGM and child marriage still happen doesn’t mean they are right—it means we haven’t done enough to stop it. Yes, Culture gives identity but it isn't static should grow and improve, not be used to justify harm. The vulnerable need protection, it is worse that it is even commercialized
